questions.q: How does JavaScript determine the data type of a value?

answer.oneLiner: JS is dynamically typed and checks the type at runtime; you inspect it with the `typeof` operator.
keyPoints: `typeof` returns: number, string, boolean, undefined, object, function, symbol, bigint
Quirk: `typeof null === 'object'` (legacy bug)
Arrays are objects — use `Array.isArray()` to detect them
`instanceof` checks the prototype chain for objects

code.snippet:
typeof 42;        // 'number'
typeof 'hi';      // 'string'
typeof null;      // 'object'  (!)
typeof undefined; // 'undefined'
typeof [];        // 'object'
Array.isArray([]);// true
code.note: typeof null === 'object' is the famous gotcha

followUps: How do you check if something is an array?

questions.q: What is NaN and how do you check for it?

answer.oneLiner: NaN means 'Not a Number' — the result of an invalid numeric operation, and it's the only value not equal to itself.
keyPoints: `typeof NaN === 'number'`
`NaN === NaN` is false — use `Number.isNaN()` to test
Produced by things like `0/0` or `Number('abc')`

code.snippet:
Number('abc');      // NaN
NaN === NaN;        // false
Number.isNaN(NaN);  // true

redFlags: Thinking NaN is its own type

questions.q: Explain hoisting for var, function declarations, and let/const (TDZ).

answer.oneLiner: Declarations are moved to the top of their scope: `var` is hoisted and initialized to undefined, function declarations are fully hoisted, and `let`/`const` are hoisted but sit in the Temporal Dead Zone until declared.
keyPoints: `var foo` → usable before its line, value is undefined
`function bar(){}` → fully usable before its line
`let`/`const` → ReferenceError if used before declaration (TDZ)
Function expressions follow their variable's hoisting rules
followUps: What is the Temporal Dead Zone?

questions.q: var vs let vs const — what are the differences?

answer.oneLiner: `var` is function-scoped and hoisted; `let` and `const` are block-scoped with a TDZ, and `const` can't be reassigned.
keyPoints: Scope: var = function, let/const = block `{}`
Redeclaration: var allows it, let/const don't
const = no reassignment (but object contents can still mutate)
Default to const, use let when reassigning, avoid var

code.note: const locks the binding, not the value

questions.q: What are template literals?

answer.oneLiner: Backtick strings that support `${}` interpolation, multi-line text, and tagged templates.
keyPoints: `` `Hello ${name}` `` embeds expressions
Multi-line without `\n` concatenation
Tagged templates let a function process the parts

questions.q: What is the difference between null and undefined?

answer.oneLiner: `undefined` means a variable was declared but never assigned (set by JS); `null` is an intentional 'no value' that you assign.
keyPoints: `undefined` = absence by default; `null` = absence on purpose
`null == undefined` is true, `null === undefined` is false
`typeof undefined === 'undefined'`, `typeof null === 'object'`

code.snippet:
let a;            // undefined
let b = null;     // null
null == undefined;// true
null === undefined;// false

questions.q: How does the `this` keyword behave in regular vs arrow functions?

answer.oneLiner: Regular functions bind `this` by how they're called (call-site); arrow functions have no own `this` — they inherit it from the enclosing scope (lexical).
keyPoints: Regular: `this` = the object before the dot, or undefined/global if called plain
Arrow: `this` is fixed to where it was defined
`call`/`apply`/`bind` change a regular function's `this`, not an arrow's
Use arrows for callbacks to keep the outer `this`
followUps: What does this point to in a plain function call?

questions.q: What are higher-order functions?

answer.oneLiner: Functions that take other functions as arguments and/or return a function — the basis of functional programming in JS.
keyPoints: `map`, `filter`, `reduce` are classic examples
Enable composition and reuse
A function returning a function (e.g. a closure factory) also qualifies

code.note: map takes a function; adder returns one

questions.q: What is automatic type conversion (coercion) in JavaScript?

answer.oneLiner: JS auto-converts types when operators or comparisons require it — implicit coercion that drives many surprising results.
keyPoints: `+` with a string concatenates; other math operators convert to number
`==` coerces types, `===` does not — prefer `===`
Falsy values: 0, '', null, undefined, NaN, false
`[] + {}` → '[object Object]' (array → '' , object → string)

code.snippet:
'5' + 1;   // '51' (string concat)
'5' - 1;   // 4    (numeric)
0 == '';   // true (coerced)
0 === '';  // false

questions.q: What is strict mode and how do you enable it?

answer.oneLiner: A safer JS mode enabled with `'use strict';` that turns silent mistakes into errors and disables some sloppy behaviour.
keyPoints: Add `'use strict';` at top of file or function
Prevents accidental globals (assigning to undeclared vars throws)
`this` is undefined in plain function calls instead of global
ES modules and class bodies are strict by default

code.snippet:
'use strict';
x = 10; // ReferenceError: x is not defined
code.note: Undeclared assignment throws in strict mode

questions.q: What is a closure? Show a counter example.

answer.oneLiner: A closure is a function that remembers and accesses variables from its outer scope even after that outer function has returned.
keyPoints: Inner function keeps a live reference to outer variables
Enables data privacy / encapsulation
Common uses: counters, factories, memoization, event handlers

code.note: Output: 1 then 2 — count is private and persists

answer.analogy: Like a backpack the function carries with the variables it needs.
followUps: How do closures cause memory leaks?

questions.q: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy?

answer.oneLiner: A shallow copy duplicates only the top level (nested objects are shared by reference); a deep copy recursively clones everything so nothing is shared.
keyPoints: Shallow: spread `{...obj}`, `Object.assign`, `Array.slice`
Deep: `structuredClone(obj)` (modern) or `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj))`
Shallow copy + mutating a nested object affects the original
JSON trick drops functions, undefined, Dates → use structuredClone

code.note: Shallow shares nested objects; deep does not

redFlags: JSON method for objects with functions/Dates

questions.q: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?

answer.oneLiner: Both store key/value strings per origin; localStorage persists until cleared, sessionStorage lasts only for the browser tab/session.
keyPoints: localStorage: survives tab/browser restart
sessionStorage: cleared when the tab closes, not shared across tabs
~5-10MB limit, strings only (JSON.stringify objects)
Synchronous, sent on same-origin; not sent to server like cookies

questions.q: What is the difference between ViewState and SessionState? (web concepts)

answer.oneLiner: ViewState stores page-level data on the client (in a hidden field, per page); SessionState stores per-user data on the server across requests.
keyPoints: ViewState: client-side, single page, travels in the page payload
SessionState: server-side, whole session, keyed by a session cookie
ViewState bloats page size; SessionState consumes server memory
ASP.NET WebForms concept — modern SPAs use localStorage/state libs instead
answer.analogy: ViewState = sticky note on the page; SessionState = file the server keeps about you.

questions.q: What is the difference between __proto__ and prototype?

answer.oneLiner: `prototype` is a property on constructor functions used as the blueprint for new instances; `__proto__` is the actual link on an instance pointing to its prototype.
keyPoints: `Fn.prototype` → object that instances inherit from
`instance.__proto__ === Fn.prototype`
`__proto__` is the chain JS walks during lookups (prototype chain)
Prefer `Object.getPrototypeOf()` over `__proto__`

code.note: Output: true — instance links to constructor's prototype

redFlags: Saying every object has a prototype property (only functions do)

questions.q: What are the different ways to create an object in JavaScript?

answer.oneLiner: Object literal, constructor function with `new`, ES6 `class`, `Object.create()`, and factory functions.
keyPoints: Literal: `{ key: val }` — most common
`new Constructor()` or `class` for many similar instances
`Object.create(proto)` to set the prototype explicitly
Factory function returning an object

code.snippet:
const a = { x: 1 };                 // literal
class C { constructor(){ this.x=1; } }
const b = new C();                  // class
const c = Object.create(null);      // no prototype

questions.q: How do you iterate over the properties of an object?

answer.oneLiner: Use `Object.keys/values/entries` with array methods, or `for...in` (which also walks inherited enumerable keys).
keyPoints: `Object.keys(obj).forEach(...)` — own enumerable keys
`Object.entries(obj)` → `[key, value]` pairs, great with for...of
`for...in` includes inherited keys — guard with `hasOwnProperty`
`for...of` works on arrays/iterables, not plain objects

questions.q: Explain the spread operator and destructuring.

answer.oneLiner: Spread (`...`) expands an iterable/object into elements; destructuring unpacks values from arrays/objects into variables.
keyPoints: Spread copies/merges: `[...a, ...b]`, `{...x, ...y}` (shallow)
Destructuring: `const { id } = user`, `const [first] = arr`
Rest collects the leftovers: `const [head, ...tail] = arr`
Defaults + renaming: `const { name: n = 'NA' } = obj`

questions.q: What is a pure function?

answer.oneLiner: A function that, for the same inputs, always returns the same output and has no side effects (no mutating external state, no I/O).
keyPoints: Deterministic — same args → same result
No side effects (no mutation, no DB/network, no console)
Easy to test, cache, and reason about
Core idea behind React reducers and memoization

code.note: add is pure; impure mutates external state

questions.q: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript and how do you avoid them?

answer.oneLiner: Leaks happen when references are unintentionally kept alive so the garbage collector can't reclaim memory.
keyPoints: Forgotten timers/intervals and event listeners not removed
Lingering closures holding large objects
Detached DOM nodes still referenced in JS
Accidental globals; growing caches/maps — use WeakMap

code.note: Always clean up timers and listeners

followUps: How would you find a leak in DevTools?

questions.q: Predict the output: var a=20; Abc(); function Abc(){ var a=10; console.log(a); }

answer.oneLiner: It logs 10 — the inner `var a` is function-scoped and shadows the outer one, and `Abc` runs because function declarations are hoisted.
keyPoints: Function declaration is hoisted, so calling before its line works
Inner `var a=10` creates a separate, function-scoped variable
Outer `a=20` is untouched

questions.q: Predict the output: var a=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,0]; a[40]=40; console.log(a.length);

answer.oneLiner: It logs 41 — assigning to index 40 makes a sparse array, and `length` is always highest index + 1.
keyPoints: length = largest index + 1, not the count of elements
Indexes 10-39 are empty 'holes' (sparse array)
Accessing a hole returns undefined

questions.q: Explain the event loop, call stack, and the task vs microtask queues.

answer.oneLiner: JS runs synchronous code on a single call stack; when it's empty the event loop drains all microtasks (promises) first, then one macrotask (timers/events), and repeats.
keyPoints: Call stack runs sync code top to bottom
Microtasks: promise `.then`, `queueMicrotask`, `await` continuations
Macrotasks: setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events
After each macrotask, ALL queued microtasks run before the next macrotask

code.note: Output: A, D, C, B (sync, then microtask, then macrotask)

answer.analogy: Sync work first; promises are the express lane drained before the next timer.

questions.q: Predict the order: setTimeout(()=>console.log(2),0); console.log(5);

answer.oneLiner: It prints 5 then 2 — synchronous `console.log(5)` runs first, and the setTimeout callback is a macrotask deferred until the stack is empty, even with a 0ms delay.
keyPoints: setTimeout schedules a callback; it never runs immediately
Sync code always finishes before any queued callback
0ms means 'as soon as possible after sync', not 'now'

code.snippet:
setTimeout(() => console.log(2), 0);
console.log(5);

questions.q: Compare callbacks, promises, and observables.

answer.oneLiner: Callbacks are functions passed in to run later (prone to nesting); promises represent one future value with chaining; observables are streams of many values over time that you can cancel.
keyPoints: Callback: simple but leads to 'callback hell' / inversion of control
Promise: single value, `.then/.catch` or async/await, not cancellable
Observable (RxJS): 0..N values, lazy, cancellable, rich operators
Promise resolves once; observable can emit repeatedly

questions.q: Is JavaScript single-threaded? How does it handle concurrency?

answer.oneLiner: Yes — JS executes on a single main thread with one call stack, but it achieves concurrency by offloading async work to the runtime and using the event loop.
keyPoints: One main thread = one thing runs at a time
Timers, network, and I/O are handled by the browser/Node, not JS itself
Web Workers add real parallel threads (separate, no shared DOM)
Long sync work blocks the UI — keep tasks small or offload
answer.analogy: One chef (thread), but oven timers (browser APIs) cook in the background.
followUps: When would you reach for a Web Worker?

questions.q: What are Web Workers and when would you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Web Workers run scripts on a separate background thread so heavy computation doesn't freeze the UI; they communicate with the main thread via messages.
keyPoints: True parallelism on a different thread
No DOM access; communicate via `postMessage` / `onmessage`
Good for CPU-heavy work: parsing, image processing, crypto
Data is copied (structured clone) unless using transferables

code.note: Offload heavy work, talk via messages

questions.q: How would you build an app that works offline?

answer.oneLiner: Use a Service Worker to cache the app shell and API responses, store data locally (IndexedDB), and sync changes back when the connection returns.
keyPoints: Service Worker intercepts requests and serves from cache (PWA)
Cache Storage for assets; IndexedDB for structured data
Detect status with `navigator.onLine` and online/offline events
Queue mutations offline and replay with Background Sync

code.note: Cache-first strategy in a service worker

questions.q: What is method chaining and how do you enable it?

answer.oneLiner: Calling multiple methods in sequence on the same object — each method returns `this` (or a new object) so the next call can continue.
keyPoints: Each method returns the object to chain on
Array methods chain because map/filter return new arrays
Builds fluent, readable APIs (e.g. jQuery, builders)

code.snippet:
[1,2,3,4]
  .filter(n => n % 2)
  .map(n => n * 10); // [10, 30]

class B { a(){return this;} b(){return this;} }
code.note: Output: [10, 30] — return this/new object to chain

questions.q: Predict the output of an async/await + promise ordering snippet.

answer.oneLiner: Logs Start, End, Promise, Timeout — sync runs first, the await continuation is a microtask (before the timer), and setTimeout is a macrotask last.
keyPoints: Code after `await` runs as a microtask
Microtasks (promise) beat macrotasks (setTimeout)
Synchronous logs print before any async continuation

questions.q: Why use React, and what is the Virtual DOM?

answer.oneLiner: React is a declarative component library that uses a Virtual DOM — an in-memory tree it diffs against the previous render to update the real DOM efficiently.
keyPoints: Declarative: describe UI for a state, React syncs the DOM
Virtual DOM = lightweight JS copy of the real DOM
Component-based, reusable, large ecosystem
Minimizes expensive direct DOM manipulation
answer.analogy: Like editing a draft (VDOM) then only applying the changed edits to the published page (DOM).

questions.q: How does React's DOM diffing / reconciliation work?

answer.oneLiner: On a state change React builds a new Virtual DOM, diffs it against the old one with a fast heuristic algorithm, and applies only the minimal set of real DOM updates.
keyPoints: Compares trees level by level (O(n) heuristic, not full diff)
Different element type → tear down and rebuild that subtree
Lists use `key` to track which items moved/changed
Bad/missing keys cause wrong reuse and bugs
redFlags: Using array index as key for reorderable lists
Saying VDOM is faster than DOM always

questions.q: What is useMemo and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: `useMemo` caches the result of an expensive calculation and only recomputes it when its dependencies change.
keyPoints: Returns a memoized VALUE, recomputed when deps change
Use for genuinely expensive computations or stable references
Don't overuse — it has its own cost and adds complexity
`useCallback` is `useMemo` for functions
redFlags: Wrapping everything in useMemo by default

questions.q: What is useRef used for?

answer.oneLiner: `useRef` returns a mutable `.current` container that persists across renders without causing a re-render — used for DOM access and storing mutable values.
keyPoints: Access DOM nodes: `<input ref={inputRef} />`
Store mutable values (timer IDs, previous values) without re-rendering
Changing `.current` does NOT trigger a render
Value survives re-renders, unlike a normal variable

questions.q: What is the difference between useMemo and useRef?

answer.oneLiner: `useMemo` caches a computed value and recomputes it when dependencies change; `useRef` holds a mutable box that persists across renders and never recomputes on its own.
keyPoints: useMemo: dependency-driven, returns a value, for expensive calc
useRef: no dependencies, manual `.current` updates
Neither triggers a re-render when its stored value changes
useMemo recalculates; useRef just remembers

code.snippet:
const v = useMemo(() => calc(a), [a]); // recomputes on a change
const r = useRef(0);                    // you mutate r.current
code.note: useMemo recomputes by deps; useRef is a manual mutable box

questions.q: What is the Context API and when would you use it?

answer.oneLiner: Context lets you share data through the component tree without passing props at every level — ideal for delivering values to a deeply nested child.
keyPoints: Avoids 'prop drilling' through intermediate components
`createContext`, wrap with `<Provider value={...}>`, read with `useContext`
Good for theme, auth/user, locale — relatively stable values
Every consumer re-renders when the value changes — split contexts if hot

code.note: Provider at top, useContext anywhere below

redFlags: Using Context for high-frequency state (causes wide re-renders)

questions.q: How do you share data between components in React?

answer.oneLiner: Pass props down for parent→child, lift state to a common ancestor for siblings, use Context for deep trees, and a state library (Redux/Zustand) for large shared state.
keyPoints: Parent → child: props
Child → parent: callback props
Siblings: lift state up to the common parent
Far/global: Context API or a state library
answer.analogy: Props = handing a note down; Context = a noticeboard everyone can read.
followUps: When do you graduate from Context to Redux?

questions.q: What is the difference between AOT and JIT compilation?

answer.oneLiner: AOT (Ahead-Of-Time) compiles code at build time before it ships; JIT (Just-In-Time) compiles at runtime in the browser.
keyPoints: AOT: faster startup, smaller bundle, errors caught at build
JIT: slower first load, larger bundle, compiles in the browser
Mostly an Angular distinction (AOT is the default in prod)
React doesn't 'compile templates' the same way — JSX is transpiled by the bundler
answer.note: Primarily an Angular concept; React ships pre-transpiled JS.

questions.q: Why doesn't React use direct DOM manipulation, and when is it still needed?

answer.oneLiner: Direct DOM updates are slow and error-prone, so React batches changes through the Virtual DOM; you only drop to the real DOM (via refs) for things React can't express declaratively.
keyPoints: React batches and minimizes real DOM writes
Manual DOM use cases: focus, scroll, measuring size, media playback, 3rd-party libs
Use `useRef` to reach a DOM node imperatively
Mixing manual DOM edits with React state causes inconsistency
redFlags: Calling document.getElementById to mutate React-managed nodes
